chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
10 PRINT "HELLO WORLD"
20 FOR I=1 TO 10
30 PRINT I; I*I
40 NEXT I
50 GOTO 10

Ok
RUN
HELLO WORLD
1  1
2  4
3  9
chunks.heading: Where It All Began
chunks.bodyHtml: GW-BASIC — bundled with IBM PCs and MS-DOS — was the very first screen that ever blinked back at an entire generation. Descended from Altair BASIC (1975), it was an interpreter: type, RUN, obey. No compiler, no build step. It taught millions that a computer was a thing you could talk to.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: The Immediate-Feedback Machine
chunks.bodyHtml: GW-BASIC's genius was instant gratification. Line numbers were the file system, the version control, the whole mental model. GOTO, GOSUB, PEEK/POKE: raw, unfiltered control over the hardware, with built-in graphics and sound. A teenager alone in a bedroom could make a game and watch it work in seconds.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: The First Spark
chunks.bodyHtml: GW-BASIC was my "hello, world" — the moment a glowing green screen became a canvas. Today I design chips where a signal flip takes weeks to verify, but the instinct is the same: formulate, run, read the output, iterate. The loop times got longer, the dopamine hit of "it works!" was forged right here.

title: dBASE III+

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
dBASE III+ · Command Mode
. USE STUDENT
. LIST NEXT 5
Record#NAMEGRADE
1HEVANGELA
2ALANB+
3GRACEA
._ █
chunks.heading: The Data Awakens
chunks.bodyHtml: dBASE was the killer app of the pre-Windows business world. Born as Vulcan (1978), it turned the IBM PC into a database machine. Its terse dot-prompt language shipped real accounting and inventory systems. It quietly invented the 4GL — describe what you want the data to do, not how.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: Persistent Records & the Dot Prompt
chunks.bodyHtml: A .DBF file was a real table on disk — fields, types, records. You could index it and query it relationally decades before SQL was common on PCs. The xBase dialect (FoxPro, Clipper) outlived Ashton-Tate itself.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: Learning That Data Has Shape
chunks.bodyHtml: dBASE taught me that programming isn't only about logic — it's about information. A record has a schema, a lifecycle; you index for speed. Today, when I debug cache-coherency bugs that span firmware, OS, and silicon, every one is at heart a data integrity problem. I started asking "what is canonical?" at an amber dot prompt.

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
FileEditRunCompileDebug
// fibonacci.cpp
#include

int fib(int n) {
  return n < 2 ? n : fib(n-1)+fib(n-2);
}
● ReadyLine 4 Col 18
chunks.heading: C with Classes
chunks.bodyHtml: C++ began in 1979 as "C with Classes" and grew into the heavyweight champion of systems programming. It gave C a layer of object orientation, templates, and RAII. Browsers, databases, game engines, OSes — and crucially, EDA tools themselves — are written in C++. It is the bedrock language of performance.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: Zero-Cost Abstraction
chunks.bodyHtml: C++ promises towering abstractions that compile down to code as tight as hand-written C. "You don't pay for what you don't use." It's the language for when performance is non-negotiable.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: Building the Tools That Build Chips
chunks.bodyHtml: The simulators and synthesizers I use every day — the tools that model a billion-transistor chip — are themselves C++ programs. Writing C++ taught me to design fast, layered systems, which is exactly the mindset RTL demands. When I optimize a datapath for timing, I'm thinking in the same cache-lines-and-throughput mental model C++ beat into me.

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
☕ HelloWorld — Applet Viewer— □ ✕
FileEditRunHelp
public class Hello extends Applet {
  public void paint(Graphics g) {
    g.drawString("Hello, Web!", 50, 25);
  }
}
Applet startedJVM 1.0 · 16 MB
chunks.heading: Write Once, Run Anywhere
chunks.bodyHtml: Java landed in 1995 like a meteor. Repackaged for the Web with mascot Duke and a steaming coffee cup, its pitch was almost religious: compile to a virtual machine and run on any device. Applets put code inside the browser; then Java quietly conquered the server, where it rules enterprise back-ends to this day.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: The JVM & Garbage Collection
chunks.bodyHtml: Java's gifts were the JVM — abstracting the OS, pioneering JIT — and automatic garbage collection as a default. Spring, Hadoop, Kafka, Android all run on its descendants.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: Abstraction as Leverage
chunks.bodyHtml: Java taught me that abstraction is leverage, not laziness. GC freed my brain to think about behavior, not memory — exactly the headroom I need to reason about a billion-transistor chip. But Java also taught me the cost of abstraction: every layer hides latency. In silicon, those hidden costs are nanoseconds and watts.

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
https://dev.local/app.js
1// the web woke up
2async function fetchUser(id) {
3  const res = await fetch(`/api/u/${id}`);
4  return res.json();
5}
6fetchUser(42).then(u => console.log(u));
chunks.heading: Brendan Eich's Ten Days
chunks.bodyHtml: JavaScript was written in ten days in 1995 — originally Mocha, renamed to ride Java's coattails, with nothing to do with Java. A joke for a decade, then Ajax and Gmail proved the browser was a real platform. With Node.js it escaped the browser and became the most widely deployed runtime in history.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: The Event Loop & Universal Reach
chunks.bodyHtml: JS's superpower is its single-threaded event loop — a model built for waiting. Its other superpower is ubiquity: every device has a JS engine. One language, front to back.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: Thinking in Events, End to End
chunks.bodyHtml: JS rewired my brain to think in events and reactions: a promise resolves, a clock edge arrives, a signal settles — the next stage reacts. It also made me a full-stack generalist. Holding the speed of the web and the rigor of silicon in one head keeps me humble — and this very page is hand-written JS.

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
⚡ waveform.vcd — sim_top▶ 100 ns/div
clk
reset_n
count[7:0]
done
Simulation: 600 nsPASSED · 0 errors
chunks.heading: Describing Silicon Before It Exists
chunks.bodyHtml: VHDL — IEEE 1076, 1987 — is one of two languages used to design every digital chip on Earth. The mind-flip: it's not a program that runs — it's a description of a circuit that exists. Write c <= a and b; and you've specified a physical AND gate etched into silicon.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: Concurrency, Signals & Simulation
chunks.bodyHtml: Everything in VHDL happens in parallel — because that's how hardware works. You simulate before fabrication because you can't patch silicon. A bug costs millions and months.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: My Day Job, My Native Layer
chunks.bodyHtml: VHDL is where my journey converges. Everything above is built on hardware described in languages like this. When a bug spans firmware, OS, and silicon, I chase it from RTL waveform up to the JS console and back. Fifty years in, the loop is closed: the kid who typed GOTO 10 now designs the gates that fetch it.

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
; bootloader.asm — 16-bit real mode
0x7C00  mov ax, 0x07C0
0x7C03  mov ds, ax
0x7C05  mov si, msg
0x7C08  call print
0x7C0B  cli ; hlt

AX=07C0 BX=0000 IP=7C0B
> halted_█
chunks.heading: The Machine's Native Tongue
chunks.bodyHtml: Assembly is the human-readable shadow of the CPU's actual instruction set. Each maps almost one-to-one onto the binary opcodes the silicon executes. Before compilers were trustworthy, it was how everything got written — OSes, games, BIOS routines.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: Total Control, Zero Safety Net
chunks.bodyHtml: Every register, every flag, every memory address — no GC, no type system, no bounds check. Its specialty is transparency: reading disassembly is how you learn what a compiler really did. The basement beneath every other language here.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: Where Software Meets Silicon
chunks.bodyHtml: Assembly was the bridge that turned me from a programmer into an engineer. The day 0x7C00 lit up in a debugger, the abstraction curtain tore. Today, when a branch predictor mispredicts, the debug trail ends in instruction-level behavior — and I can draw a straight line from the RTL waveform to the disassembly. It's the Rosetta Stone between my day job and everything else.

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
♦ waveform_panel.tcl
RunStepQuit
chunks.heading: Embeddable Glue
chunks.bodyHtml: Tcl — the Tool Command Language — was created by John Ousterhout at Berkeley as a tiny, embeddable scripting engine. Pair it with Tk, its GUI toolkit, and you could build a cross-platform windowed app in a dozen lines. EDA vendors loved it — the command consoles inside virtually every chip-design tool speak Tcl.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: The Language Inside the Tools
chunks.bodyHtml: Tcl's specialty is being hosted. "Everything is a command, everything is a string." Minimalist enough to drive a tool, flexible enough to script complex flows. Tk proved GUIs didn't require C++ and a week of boilerplate.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: Driving the EDA Engines
chunks.bodyHtml: Tcl is the common tongue of chip design. Whether I'm scripting synthesis or automating place-and-route, the console is almost always Tcl. It taught me that a powerful tool is really a programmable tool — the best engineers don't click buttons, they script the engine. Every tapeout I've shipped has Tcl threading through its spine.

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
hevan@vax — /bin/tcsh— □ ✕
hevan@vax:~/tapeout% ls -la *.v
-rw-r--r--  1 hevan 4096 top.v
hevan@vax:~/tapeout% make sim | tee run.log
# compiling...
PASS — 1024 vectors, 0 fails
hevan@vax:~/tapeout% █
chunks.heading: The Pipe & the Filter
chunks.bodyHtml: The Unix shell — sh, csh, and especially tcsh on Berkeley Unix — is the original "glue language." Chain tiny tools with |: grep, sed, awk, sort. Composability as a philosophy. tcsh added filename completion, history editing, and job control.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: Composable Automation
chunks.bodyHtml: The shell's superpower is orthogonal composition: every tool reads stdin and writes stdout, so any pipeline just works. It is the duct tape that has held engineering together for fifty years.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: Automation as a Reflex
chunks.bodyHtml: The shell is where I learned that if you do it twice, script it. Every chip project I run is stitched together by tcsh and Make — nightly regressions, coverage merges, log scraping. The AI chips I help build are born inside shell scripts that kick off a thousand simulations and report back in green PASS.

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
◆ specman — cpu_test.evrun -f
' — constrained random CPU test
struct cpu_instr like base_instr {
  opcode : op_e;
  keep opcode != NOP;
  event cov_op is cover(opcode) {
    cross opcode, mode;
  };
};

// Coverage: 94.2% — temporal holes
chunks.heading: The Pioneer of Random Verification
chunks.bodyHtml: The e language, created by Yoav Hollander at Verisity (later Cadence), was the original constrained-random verification language — predating SystemVerilog/UVM by years. Its Aspect-Oriented features let engineers inject checks anywhere without touching the original code.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: Aspects & Temporal Checking
chunks.bodyHtml: e's specialties are Aspect-Oriented Programming — weave new behavior into existing code without modifying it — and powerful temporal assertions that check sequences over time. It made verification a first-class engineering discipline.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: Learning to Trust Randomness
chunks.bodyHtml: e taught me a counterintuitive truth: the best tester is one that surprises you. Hand-written tests find the bugs you imagined; random verification finds the ones you didn't. That philosophy — define the rules, then let the machine explore — is exactly how I think about AI today.

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
alu_driver.svuvm_pkg.sv
// UVM sequence drives the ALU
class alu_seq extends uvm_sequence;
  task body();
    req.op = ADD;
    start_item(req); finish_item(req);
  endtask
}

// Coverage: 98.7% — 2 holes in flag paths
chunks.heading: One Language, Two Jobs
chunks.bodyHtml: SystemVerilog (IEEE 1800) merged Verilog's design syntax with a powerful OOP verification layer. Then UVM made it possible to build reusable, randomized test environments for the most complex chips ever built. It is the industry standard for taping out modern silicon.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: Constrained-Random Verification
chunks.bodyHtml: SystemVerilog's superpower is constrained random testing: describe the legal input space, and the solver blasts millions of edge-case scenarios at your design, hunting bugs no human would think to write. Combined with functional coverage, it turns "did we test enough?" into a measurable number.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: Proving the Chip Before It's Real
chunks.bodyHtml: SystemVerilog + UVM is how I sleep at night before a tapeout. On an AI accelerator, you can't test every MAC by hand — you let the randomized environment find the one-in-a-billion collision. Verification is harder than design, and coverage is a moral contract with the silicon.

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
◆ irb — interactive ruby
irb> [1,2,3].map { |n| n**2 }
=> [1, 4, 9]
irb> :silicon.class
=> Symbol
irb> class Chip
irb>   attr_accessor :name
irb> █
chunks.heading: Optimized for Joy
chunks.bodyHtml: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto designed Ruby with one goal: programmer happiness. Everything is an object. Blocks, closures, and a syntax that reads almost like English. When Rails arrived in 2004, Ruby became the fastest way to ship a web app, and a generation of startups was born on it.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: Expressiveness & DSLs
chunks.bodyHtml: Ruby's specialty is malleability — you can bend its syntax into a domain-specific language. Rails reads like declarative poetry: has_many :comments. It prioritized developer flow over machine speed, a radical idea at the time.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: The Value of Beauty
chunks.bodyHtml: Ruby taught me that code can be beautiful, and beauty matters. After years of terse C and rigid Java, Ruby's expressiveness felt like oxygen. It trained me to value readability and developer experience — principles I now apply to RTL testbenches and build scripts. Elegance is a form of engineering discipline, not vanity.

dividerBefore: the era of glue and data
title: Python

chunks.label: ▸ 01 / History
chunks.visualHtml:
🐍 python3 — REPL3.12.0
>>> def softmax(x):
...    e = np.exp(x - x.max())
...    return e / e.sum()
>>> softmax([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
array([0.09, 0.24, 0.67])
>>> # the lingua franca of AI
>>> █
chunks.heading: Batteries Included
chunks.bodyHtml: Python, named after Monty Python, was built to be readable above all. NumPy, Pandas, and PyTorch made it the lingua franca of AI. Today, every neural network training run on Earth begins with import torch.

chunks.label: ▸ 02 / Specialty
chunks.heading: The Universal Glue
chunks.bodyHtml: Python's specialty is breadth: it wraps C/C++/Fortran libraries, scripts EDA flows, runs web servers, and trains trillion-parameter models. Readable enough for a beginner, powerful enough to run the world's AI.

chunks.label: ▸ 03 / My Take
chunks.heading: The Bridge to AI Silicon
chunks.bodyHtml: Python is the bridge between my two worlds. The AI models that my chips accelerate are defined and trained in Python; the scripts that verify those same chips are Python too. The engineer who speaks both PyTorch and RTL is the one who can actually ship the silicon that powers it. Python closed that loop for me.
